Title: Evidence for evolution and selection of drought-resistant genes based on high-throughput resequencing in weedy rice

Plant_Genes.Notes_or_Other_Important_Info: PAPH1 is located in a de-domestication-related selective sweep on chromosome 1 and shares haplotypes between weedy rice and both landrace and improved rice, supporting gene flow during the evolution of weedy rice. The gene spans 7303 bp and was edited at the SG site between 437–456 bp. The study emphasizes that PAPH1 is an example of a drought-resistance gene that has been enriched during weedy rice evolution and can be directly used in improving cultivated rice.
Plant_Genes.Summary_key_Findings_of_Core_Gene:
1. Selection and GWAS analyses using whole-genome resequencing of 501 accessions and drought traits in 274 japonica-type accessions identified Os01g0800500 (PAPH1) within a chromosome 1 region under strong selection and associated with leaf withering degree under drought.
2. PAPH1 encodes a purple acid phosphatase-family protein; knockout via CRISPR/Cas9 in weedy rice accession WR16 greatly reduces drought tolerance (higher leaf withering degree), while overexpression markedly enhances drought tolerance (almost no leaf withering) under severe drought.
3. Under severe drought, PAPH1 expression is strongly decreased in edited lines and increased in overexpression lines; in parallel, loss of PAPH1 causes Ca2+ efflux and reduced K+ influx in mesophyll cells, whereas overexpression increases both Ca2+ and K+ influx, indicating a role in regulating ion flux homeostasis.
4. Two japonica-type PAPH1 haplotypes (Hap1 and Hap2) are widely distributed among weedy, landrace, and improved rice and are each associated with high drought-resistance index D, suggesting that PAPH1 haplotypes and alleles selected during de-domestication can be valuable genetic resources for breeding drought-resistant cultivated rice.
Plant_Genes.Omics_Data: Whole-genome resequencing of 501 rice accessions (data deposited in NCBI SRA under accession SRP356306) used for selection scans, GWAS, and haplotype analysis of PAPH1; previous transcriptome, proteome, and PRM analyses in WR16 identified the PAPH1-encoded protein (Os01T0800500-01) as associated with strong drought resistance.
Plant_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PAPH1 positively regulates drought resistance; loss of function increases leaf withering and reduces drought tolerance, whereas overexpression markedly enhances drought tolerance in weedy rice.
Plant_Genes.Regulatory_Mechanism: By encoding a PAP-family phosphatase, PAPH1 modulates Ca2+ and K+ fluxes across mesophyll cell membranes under drought; loss of PAPH1 causes Ca2+ efflux and reduced K+ influx, whereas overexpression leads to increased Ca2+ and K+ influx, indicating a role in maintaining cellular ion homeostasis during drought stress.
Plant_Genes.Expression_Pattern: Under severe drought at the meiosis stage, PAPH1 transcript levels are strongly reduced in CRISPR-edited knockout lines and strongly elevated in overexpression lines compared with their respective wild-type controls.
Plant_Genes.Key_Variant_Site: CRISPR/Cas9-edited single-nucleotide (A) insertion at positions 437–456 bp within the PAPH1 coding region (genic), generating a loss-of-function allele associated with weak drought tolerance (LWD score 7 vs 3 in wild type), and two natural japonica-type haplotypes (Hap1: CGTGTCGGTCGCGAGGATC; Hap2: CGAACCGGTTGAAAGAAAC) within the PAPH1 genic region, whose carriers show high drought-resistance index D; variants are genic and reported as affecting drought-resistance function.
Plant_Genes.Key_Variant_Type: Indel (single-base insertion, CRISPR-induced) and SNP-defined haplotypes (Hap1, Hap2) within the PAPH1 gene
Plant_Genes.Reference_Genome_Version: Nipponbare (Oryza sativa ssp. japonica) reference genome
Plant_Genes.Key_Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Severe drought stress imposed by stopping irrigation in field trials and by withholding water from potted WR16 plants at the meiosis stage until soil water content reached ~4.1%, when flag leaves were strongly drought-stressed and withering.
Plant_Genes.Research_Topic: Evolution and selection of drought-resistance genes during de-domestication of weedy rice, and functional characterization of PAPH1 as a drought-resistance gene affecting Ca2+ and K+ flux.
Plant_Genes.Trait_Category: abiotic stress – drought resistance; cellular ion homeostasis (Ca2+ and K+ flux) under drought
Plant_Genes.Favorable_Allele: Functional PAPH1 alleles, including japonica-type Hap1 and Hap2 and high-expression/overexpression alleles, are associated with strong drought resistance (low leaf withering scores and high D index), whereas the CRISPR-induced insertion allele is unfavorable, conferring weak drought tolerance.
Plant_Genes.Superior_Haplotype: PAPH1 japonica-type Hap1 and Hap2 haplotypes: in GWAS accessions ~85% of Hap1 carriers and ~77% of Hap2 carriers showed strong drought resistance based on the comprehensive index D, with top D values (up to 0.905 for Hap1 and 0.817 for Hap2) exceeding that of the drought-resistant cultivar IAPAR-9.
Plant_Genes.Quantitative_Phenotypic_Alterations: Under severe drought, PAPH1 expression in knockout (ED) plants was ~27% of wild type (WTE), while in overexpression (OE) lines it was 3.73-fold higher than wild type (WTO). Leaf withering degree (LWD) scores were 7 in ED vs 3 in WTE, and 1 in OE vs 3 in WTO. Ca2+ net flux in mesophyll cells changed from ~0 pmol cm^-2 s^-1 in WTE/WTO to about −100 pmol cm^-2 s^-1 in ED and +100 pmol cm^-2 s^-1 in OE. K+ net flux changed from ~100 pmol cm^-2 s^-1 in wild types to ~0 pmol cm^-2 s^-1 in ED and ~500 pmol cm^-2 s^-1 in OE.
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Materials: Weedy rice accession WR16 (S705) background; T2-generation CRISPR/Cas9 PAPH1 knockout (ED) lines; T2-generation PAPH1 overexpression (OE) transgenic lines constructed with pTCK303; wild-type controls WTE and WTO.
Plant_Genes.Variety: weedy rice accession WR16 (S705) used as the genetic background for PAPH1 knockout and overexpression
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Methods: Whole-genome resequencing and SNP calling; selective sweep analysis using Fst and XP-CLR; GWAS for drought-related traits including leaf withering degree; design of gRNA and CRISPR/Cas9-mediated gene editing of PAPH1 in WR16; Agrobacterium-mediated transformation to generate knockout and PAPH1-overexpression lines; selection of T2 plants; drought treatment by withholding water at meiosis; phenotyping of leaf withering degree using a 1–9 scale; RNA extraction and qRT-PCR for PAPH1 expression; non-invasive microelectrode technology (NMT) to measure Ca2+ and K+ fluxes in mesophyll cells under severe drought.
Plant_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9 knockout of PAPH1 and construction of PAPH1 overexpression lines in WR16, followed by drought-stress phenotyping (leaf withering degree), qRT-PCR expression analysis, and Ca2+ and K+ ion flux assays using NMT in flag leaf mesophyll cells under severe drought.
Plant_Genes.Field_Trials: Field experiments at the Hainan Experimental Base in 2017 and 2018 with 274 japonica-type accessions; irrigation stopped 20 days after transplanting to impose drought, and drought-related traits including leaf withering degree were measured for GWAS.
Plant_Genes.Genetic_Population: 274 japonica-type rice accessions (88 weedy rice, 94 improved rice, 92 landrace rice) evaluated for eight drought-related traits and used in GWAS to map loci including the region containing PAPH1.
Plant_Genes.Gene_Discovery_or_Cloning_Method: Identified as a drought-tolerance candidate by combining genome-wide selection scans (Fst and XP-CLR between japonica weedy rice and cultivated rice), GWAS for leaf withering degree under field drought, and multi-omics data (transcriptome, proteome, PRM) from WR16; then functionally tested via CRISPR/Cas9 knockout and overexpression in WR16.
Plant_Genes.Breeding_Application_Value: PAPH1 is a drought-resistance gene from weedy rice that enhances tolerance by improving leaf survival and maintaining Ca2+ and K+ influx under severe drought; its favorable haplotypes and functional alleles can be exploited as donors or markers in breeding programs to improve drought resistance in cultivated rice, and PAPH1 overexpression or precise editing could be used to develop varieties with reduced leaf withering and better performance under water deficit.
